title: OCCT warm-start support
analysis:
Task 28 requires implementing the WarmStartable trait (from reify-types) for both OcctKernel (raw, !Send+!Sync) and OcctKernelHandle (Send+Sync channel wrapper). The core challenge is that cxx::UniquePtr<OcctShape> is !Send, so TopoDS_Shape instances cannot be placed directly into OpaqueState (which requires Box<dyn Any + Send>). The solution is to serialize shapes to BRep format (OCCT's native ASCII B-Rep representation) via BRepTools::Write/Read on the C++ side. The serialized Strings are Send-safe and can flow through OpaqueState and the WarmStatePool.

The warm state captures the full kernel state: a HashMap<u64, String> mapping handle IDs to BRep strings, plus the next_id counter. On restore, shapes are deserialized back into TopoDS_Shape on the kernel thread. For OcctKernelHandle, two new channel request variants (WarmState and WithWarmState) forward the trait methods to the dedicated kernel thread, following the existing request/reply pattern.

TKBRep is already linked in build.rs so no build changes are needed — just new #include for BRepTools.hxx and BRep_Builder.hxx in the C++ wrapper. The existing FFI error handling pattern (catch Standard_Failure -> std::runtime_error) applies to the new serialize/deserialize functions.

All changes are confined to crates/reify-kernel-occt. The WarmStartable trait and OpaqueState are already defined in reify-types and exported. The cache.rs warm_state field and WarmStatePool in reify-runtime are already wired — this task only needs to make OcctKernel/Handle produce and consume warm state.

**Post-review update:** Reviewer identified that with_warm_state() calls self.shapes.clear() before deserialization, meaning if all BRep deserializations fail (e.g., corrupted warm state data), the kernel is left with an empty shapes map and next_id advanced — irrecoverably destroying pre-existing state. The fix is to deserialize into a staging HashMap first, then swap atomically only if at least one shape was successfully restored.

prerequisites.description: Add BRep serialize/deserialize FFI functions to C++ wrapper (occt_wrapper.h/.cpp) and CXX bridge (ffi.rs): serialize_brep(shape) -> rust::String and deserialize_brep(data: &CxxString) -> UniquePtr<OcctShape>. Uses BRepTools::Write to std::ostringstream and BRepTools::Read from std::istringstream with BRep_Builder. Add #include <BRepTools.hxx> and <BRep_Builder.hxx> to cpp file. No build.rs changes needed (TKBRep already linked).

Define the OcctWarmState struct in lib.rs: struct OcctWarmState { shapes: HashMap<u64, String>, next_id: u64 }. This is the Send-safe payload that goes inside OpaqueState. Uses String because BRep ASCII format is valid UTF-8 text and the CXX bridge returns rust::String which converts to Rust String.

steps.description: Write test in lib.rs: BRep serialization roundtrip — create a box shape via ffi::ffi::make_box(10, 20, 30), serialize with ffi::ffi::serialize_brep, verify non-empty result. Deserialize with ffi::ffi::deserialize_brep, query volume of deserialized shape via ffi::ffi::query_volume, assert it matches original volume (~6000) within tolerance 1.0.

Implement serialize_brep and deserialize_brep in occt_wrapper.h/.cpp and ffi.rs. serialize_brep: BRepTools::Write(shape.shape, oss) to std::ostringstream, return rust::String. deserialize_brep: create BRep_Builder, std::istringstream from data, BRepTools::Read(shape.shape, iss, builder). Both catch Standard_Failure. Add to ffi.rs bridge: fn serialize_brep(shape: &OcctShape) -> Result<String>; fn deserialize_brep(data: &CxxString) -> Result<UniquePtr<OcctShape>>.
Write test in lib.rs: OcctKernel::warm_state() returns None when kernel is freshly created with no shapes stored.

Implement WarmStartable::warm_state for OcctKernel. If shapes map is empty, return None. Otherwise serialize each shape to BRep string via ffi::ffi::serialize_brep (skipping shapes that fail to serialize), collect into OcctWarmState { shapes, next_id }, wrap in OpaqueState::new() with size estimate = sum of string byte lengths + 64 bytes overhead.
Write test in lib.rs: OcctKernel::warm_state() returns Some after executing a box and cylinder. Verify estimated_size_bytes() > 0.
Verify step-5 passes with existing warm_state implementation from step-4. Fix any issues if needed.

Write test in lib.rs: OcctKernel warm-start roundtrip — create kernel A with box (10x20x30), extract warm_state(). Create fresh kernel B, call with_warm_state(state). Query volume on kernel B using handle ID 1 (the box). Assert volume matches ~6000 within tolerance.

Implement WarmStartable::with_warm_state for OcctKernel. Downcast OpaqueState to OcctWarmState via state.downcast::<OcctWarmState>(). If downcast fails, return silently (wrong type, per trait contract). Clear existing shapes, iterate warm.shapes deserializing each BRep string via ffi::ffi::deserialize_brep (skip entries that fail), insert into shapes map, set next_id from warm state.

Write test in lib.rs: full OcctKernel warm-start roundtrip with multiple shapes — create kernel with box (10x20x30), cylinder (r=5,h=20), and boolean union. Extract warm_state(). Create fresh kernel, restore with_warm_state(). Verify all three handles resolve: query volume on box (~6000), cylinder (~pi*25*20 ≈ 1570.8), union (positive). Also verify next_id restored correctly by executing a new sphere and checking its handle ID is 4.
Ensure multi-shape roundtrip works. Fix any issues with next_id restoration or handle ordering.

Write test in lib.rs: OcctKernel::with_warm_state silently ignores wrong type — call with_warm_state(OpaqueState::new(String::from("garbage"), 8)). Verify kernel still functions: execute a box and query its volume successfully.
Verify wrong-type handling works (should already pass via downcast guard in step-8). Confirm test passes.
Write test in handle.rs: OcctKernelHandle::warm_state() via channel — spawn handle, execute a box op, call warm_state() (sync), verify it returns Some with estimated_size_bytes > 0.

Add WarmState { reply: oneshot::Sender<Option<OpaqueState>> } and WithWarmState { state: OpaqueState, reply: oneshot::Sender<()> } variants to OcctRequest enum. Handle them in the kernel thread's message loop by calling kernel.warm_state() / kernel.with_warm_state(). Add sync warm_state(&self) and with_warm_state(&self, state) methods on OcctKernelHandle using blocking_send/recv. Implement WarmStartable trait for OcctKernelHandle delegating to these inherent methods.

Write test in handle.rs: cross-handle warm-start transfer — spawn handle A, execute box (10x20x30), extract warm_state(). Spawn handle B, call with_warm_state(state). Query volume on handle B using handle ID 1, verify ~6000.
Ensure cross-handle warm-start transfer works end-to-end. Fix any channel or serialization issues.

Write #[tokio::test] in handle.rs: async warm-start methods — spawn handle, execute_async box, call warm_state_async(), verify Some. Spawn new handle, call with_warm_state_async(state), query_async volume, verify ~6000.

Add warm_state_async(&self) -> Option<OpaqueState> and with_warm_state_async(&self, state: OpaqueState) methods to OcctKernelHandle using async send/recv, following the pattern of execute_async/query_async/etc.
Write test in handle.rs: warm_state() on handle with empty kernel returns None — spawn handle, immediately call warm_state() without executing any ops, verify it returns None.

Verify empty-kernel None case passes through channel correctly. Final cleanup: add doc comments to all new public methods (warm_state, with_warm_state, warm_state_async, with_warm_state_async) and the OcctWarmState struct. Ensure all existing tests still pass.

Write test in lib.rs: with_warm_state_preserves_state_on_total_deserialization_failure — create a kernel with a box (10x20x30), verify it works (query volume ~6000). Construct a corrupted OcctWarmState manually: OcctWarmState { shapes: HashMap from [(1, "INVALID_BREP_DATA".to_string()), (2, "ALSO_GARBAGE".to_string())], next_id: 99 }. Wrap in OpaqueState::new(corrupted_warm, 64). Call kernel.with_warm_state(corrupted_state). Assert the kernel's pre-existing state survives: query volume on handle ID 1 (the original box) must still return ~6000. Also verify next_id was NOT advanced to 99 by executing a new sphere and asserting its handle ID is 2 (not 99). This test should FAIL against the current implementation which clears shapes before deserialization.

Refactor with_warm_state in OcctKernel to use atomic staging: deserialize all BRep entries into a staging HashMap<u64, UniquePtr<OcctShape>> first. If the staging map is empty (all deserializations failed), return early without modifying self.shapes or self.next_id — preserving the kernel's pre-existing state. Only if at least one shape was successfully deserialized, replace self.shapes with the staging map and set self.next_id = warm.next_id. This makes the swap atomic: either the warm state is (at least partially) restored, or the kernel state is untouched.

Write test in lib.rs: with_warm_state_partial_deserialization_replaces_state — create a kernel with a box (10x20x30). Construct a partially-corrupted OcctWarmState: serialize a real cylinder (r=5,h=20) to BRep via a helper kernel, then build OcctWarmState { shapes: HashMap from [(1, valid_cylinder_brep), (2, "CORRUPT".to_string())], next_id: 10 }. Call kernel.with_warm_state(partially_corrupted). Assert: handle 1 now returns cylinder volume (~1570.8, not box volume), handle 2 query fails (shape was corrupt, not restored), and next_id was updated (new execute returns handle ID 10). This verifies partial restoration proceeds correctly when at least one shape deserializes.

Verify step-23 passes with the staged deserialization from step-22. The partial case should work because the staging map has one valid entry, so the swap proceeds. The corrupt entry is skipped, so handle 2 won't be in the map. Fix any issues if needed.

design_decisions.decision: Use BRep ASCII serialization (BRepTools::Write/Read) rather than binary (BinTools) or STEP for shape serialization
design_decisions.rationale: BRep is OCCT's native shape representation format — it preserves exact topology and geometry without loss. ASCII is debuggable and the TKBRep library is already linked. Binary would be more compact but adds complexity for minimal gain at this stage. STEP is too heavyweight (involves protocol translation) and loses some internal topological structure.

design_decisions.decision: Warm state captures entire kernel state (all shapes + next_id) rather than per-operation state
design_decisions.rationale: The OcctKernel is a stateful accumulator — shapes reference each other by handle ID (boolean ops take two handle IDs as inputs). Capturing the full shapes map preserves these cross-references. Per-operation caching would not preserve the handle ID namespace consistency needed for dependent operations.

design_decisions.decision: Use String (not Vec<u8>) for serialized BRep in OcctWarmState
design_decisions.rationale: BRep ASCII format is valid UTF-8 text. The CXX bridge naturally maps between C++ std::string / rust::String and Rust String. Using String avoids unnecessary bytes-to-string conversion at the FFI boundary and makes debugging easier (can print/inspect the BRep text).

design_decisions.decision: OcctWarmState is a private struct in the kernel-occt crate, not exposed publicly
design_decisions.rationale: The concrete warm state type is an implementation detail hidden behind OpaqueState type erasure. External code interacts only through the WarmStartable trait and OpaqueState. This encapsulates the BRep serialization format, allowing it to change (e.g., to binary) without affecting consumers.

design_decisions.decision: with_warm_state uses staged deserialization with atomic swap (revised from original clear-first approach)
design_decisions.rationale: Original implementation called self.shapes.clear() before deserialization, which irrecoverably destroys pre-existing kernel state if all BRep deserializations fail. The staged approach deserializes into a temporary HashMap first. If the staging map is empty (total failure), the kernel state is preserved untouched. Only when at least one shape deserializes successfully does the swap occur. This prevents data loss from corrupted warm state payloads while maintaining the full-replacement semantics for successful restores.

design_decisions.decision: Implement WarmStartable on both OcctKernel and OcctKernelHandle
design_decisions.rationale: OcctKernel impl contains the core serialization/deserialization logic with direct shape access. OcctKernelHandle impl forwards via channel to the kernel thread, maintaining thread-safety. Both are needed: OcctKernel for direct unit tests without channel overhead, OcctKernelHandle for production use where the kernel lives on a dedicated thread.

design_decisions.decision: Silently skip individual shapes that fail to serialize or deserialize rather than failing the entire warm-state operation
design_decisions.rationale: Per the WarmStartable trait contract, warm state is best-effort. If one shape's BRep data is somehow corrupted or its serialization fails, it's better to preserve/restore the rest than to lose everything. The evaluation engine will re-compute any missing shapes on the next demand.

design_decisions.decision: WarmStartable::with_warm_state takes &mut self on OcctKernelHandle even though channel only needs &self
design_decisions.rationale: The WarmStartable trait signature requires &mut self for with_warm_state. The handle's inherent with_warm_state method can use &self (mpsc::Sender::blocking_send only needs &self), but the trait impl must use &mut self. This is fine since the handle is typically owned by a single consumer.

reuse.how: Wrap OcctWarmState in OpaqueState::new(warm_state, size_estimate) for warm_state(). Use state.downcast::<OcctWarmState>() to recover in with_warm_state().
Implement the trait for both OcctKernel and OcctKernelHandle. No changes needed to the trait itself — it's already designed for this use case.

Add WarmState and WithWarmState variants to OcctRequest following the exact same pattern as Execute/Query/Export/Tessellate: each variant has a oneshot::Sender for reply, sync methods use blocking_send/blocking_recv, async methods use send().await/recv().await.
BRepTools::Write, BRepTools::Read, and BRep_Builder are all in TKBRep which is already in the linked libraries list. Only need to add #include directives in C++ wrapper, no build.rs changes.
serialize_brep and deserialize_brep follow the same try { ... } catch (Standard_Failure const& e) { throw std::runtime_error(...); } pattern as every other FFI function.

reuse.what: Test patterns (create box, query volume, assert tolerance)
reuse.how: Reuse the same GeometryOp::Box with Value::Real, query volume, assert (v - expected).abs() < tolerance pattern for warm-start roundtrip verification tests.
